serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of yam fritter is one piece weighing approximately 60 g, commonly sold as a street snack or hawker stall item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Yam Fritter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yam Fritter (cr_id F11090068). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this canonical Singapore source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Taro / Yam-based products — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for yam fritter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated based on the closest analogous food: cooked taro / yam (GI approximately 53–56, low category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Deep-frying and batter coating moderately increase caloric density but do not substantially raise GI relative to the base ingredient. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 21.06) / 100 ≈ 11.4 for a 60 g serving (35.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 0.60 = 21.06 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 219 mg/100 g combined with high fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece (≈60 g), contributing approximately 131 mg sodium, and avoid additional dipping sauces high in sodium; the estimated potassium content (~210 mg per serving) partially offsets sodium load in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (35.1 g/100 g) with low GI (54) and dietary fibre 3.5 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yam fritter has a low GI of 54 and provides 3.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g, which slows glucose absorption; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to one piece per occasion and account for the 21 g carbohydrate per serving within their total daily carbohydrate budget, noting that deep-frying significantly increases caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (15.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying, including saturated fat and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process introduces approximately 15.5 g total fat per 100 g including saturated fat (~2.3 g) and trace trans fat (~0.1 g), both of which are atherogenic; individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this preparation and prefer steamed or baked yam alternatives to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trace trans fat, both pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should avoid this food and opt for steamed or baked yam preparations instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yam fritter is a popular Singaporean hawker and street snack made from sliced or mashed yam (taro, Colocasia esculenta) coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is light and crunchy from the batter, while the interior is soft, starchy, and mildly sweet with the characteristic earthy flavour of yam. It is commonly sold at local coffee shops, hawker centres, and pasar malam (night markets) across Singapore, and is typically consumed as a breakfast item or afternoon snack. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 291 kcal, 2.9 g protein, 15.5 g fat, and 35.1 g carbohydrate with 3.5 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), making it more glycaemically favourable than many other fried snacks, though the deep-frying process substantially increases caloric density and introduces saturated and trace trans fats. Sodium content is moderate at 219 mg/100 g. Clinically, this food is suitable in small portions for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and fibre content, but should be limited for those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory effects of deep-frying and the presence of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly, but contains gluten from the wheat flour batter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a yam fritter?
Is yam fritter suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11090068), which is the canonical primary source for Singapore food composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on cooked taro/yam, which is the closest approved GI source entry. Multilingual names are verified for Singaporean cuisine context.
notes: sugar_g is 0.0 per HPB source data; this is confirmed zero as yam contains negligible free sugars and no added sugar is used in the standard preparation — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for yam fritter is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. trans_fat_g is reported as approximately 0.1 g/100 g based on typical deep-frying with vegetable/palm oil; this is a trace estimate consistent with standard deep-fried snack profiles. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for yam fritter. potassium_mg estimated at 350 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked taro (USDA SR Legacy item 11514); this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct HPB entry if available. saturated_fat_g estimated at 2.3 g/100 g based on typical palm/vegetable oil deep-frying profile for similar starchy snacks in USDA FoodData Central; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on cooked taro/yam (GI 53–56); no direct GI measurement for yam fritter exists in the approved GI database. The input dietary_tags 'wholegrain' is not supported for this food as the batter uses refined wheat flour; this tag has been removed. The input 'primary_cuisine' was null and has been set to Singaporean based on food_subgroup and region context.
